questions.question: Order the James-Lange sequence from stimulus to emotion:
items: Feeling (the emotion)
Bodily reaction (sweating, heart rate)
Stimulus (e.g. bear)
correct_order: Stimulus (e.g. bear)
Bodily reaction (sweating, heart rate)
Feeling (the emotion)
questions.explanation: James-Lange theory: stimulus → bodily reaction → feeling. The perception of the bodily reaction causes the emotion ('we are afraid because we run from the bear').

questions.question: Order the fear-conditioning acquisition pathway from sensory input to fear output:
items: Hypothalamus / brainstem → autonomic + behavioural fear output
Amygdala (lateral → basal → central)
Thalamus (fast subcortical relay)
Sensory input
correct_order: Sensory input
Thalamus (fast subcortical relay)
Amygdala (lateral → basal → central)
Hypothalamus / brainstem → autonomic + behavioural fear output
questions.explanation: LeDoux's 'low road': sensory input → thalamus → amygdala (lateral → basal → central) → hypothalamus / brainstem → autonomic + behavioural fear output. Cortical inputs provide a slower 'high road.'

questions.question: Order the historical theories of emotion in roughly chronological order:
items: Schachter-Singer two-factor
Papez circuit / MacLean limbic system
James-Lange
Cannon-Bard (diencephalic)
correct_order: James-Lange
Cannon-Bard (diencephalic)
Papez circuit / MacLean limbic system
Schachter-Singer two-factor
questions.explanation: Roughly chronological progression: James-Lange (late 1800s, peripheral feedback) → Cannon-Bard (1920s, thalamic) → Papez/MacLean (1930s-40s, limbic system) → Schachter-Singer (1960s, two-factor / cognitive labelling).

questions.question: Order the Iowa Gambling Task experimental sequence:
items: Compare anticipatory SCRs and choice patterns across groups
Participants make choices across ~100 trials with monetary rewards/losses
Four decks set up: A & B disadvantageous, C & D advantageous
Healthy controls develop anticipatory SCRs and shift to C & D
correct_order: Four decks set up: A & B disadvantageous, C & D advantageous
Participants make choices across ~100 trials with monetary rewards/losses
Healthy controls develop anticipatory SCRs and shift to C & D
Compare anticipatory SCRs and choice patterns across groups
questions.explanation: Iowa Gambling Task: four decks (A & B disadvantageous, C & D advantageous) → trials with rewards/losses → controls shift to C & D and produce anticipatory SCRs → group comparison reveals vmPFC patients fail to develop anticipatory SCRs and keep choosing A & B.

questions.question: Order the somatic marker mechanism from event to decision:
items: Somatic marker constrains decision options ('good' or 'bad')
Body loop or 'as-if' loop reactivates somatic pattern
Similar situation re-arises
vmPFC indexes link between factual knowledge and bioregulatory state
correct_order: vmPFC indexes link between factual knowledge and bioregulatory state
Similar situation re-arises
Body loop or 'as-if' loop reactivates somatic pattern
Somatic marker constrains decision options ('good' or 'bad')
questions.explanation: Damasio's somatic marker hypothesis: vmPFC binds factual knowledge to bodily states → similar situation arises → direct body loop or 'as-if' loop (insula, somatosensory cortex) reactivates the pattern → somatic marker acts as a heuristic constraining decision options.

questions.question: Order the McGaugh memory modulation pathway from emotional event to enhanced memory:
items: Direct amygdala projections (fast) + hormone release: (nor)epinephrine, cortisol (slow)
Enhanced consolidation in MTL, dlPFC, vlPFC
Emotionally arousing event occurs
Amygdala activation
correct_order: Emotionally arousing event occurs
Amygdala activation
Direct amygdala projections (fast) + hormone release: (nor)epinephrine, cortisol (slow)
Enhanced consolidation in MTL, dlPFC, vlPFC
questions.explanation: McGaugh's memory modulation hypothesis: emotional event → amygdala activation → fast direct projections + slow hormonal route (epinephrine, cortisol; β-adrenergic effects blocked by propranolol) → enhanced post-encoding consolidation in MTL and PFC.

questions.question: Order the cognitive reappraisal cascade across the three levels of emotion:
items: Reduced amygdala activity vs passive viewing
Reduced subjective negative-affect rating
Reappraisal goal: actively decrease negative affect from a picture
Increased dlPFC / parietal frontoparietal activity
correct_order: Reappraisal goal: actively decrease negative affect from a picture
Increased dlPFC / parietal frontoparietal activity
Reduced amygdala activity vs passive viewing
Reduced subjective negative-affect rating
questions.explanation: Cognitive reappraisal: set the goal → engage frontoparietal control (dlPFC, parietal) → downregulate amygdala → reduce subjective negative affect. The strategy operates at all three levels of emotion (neural, autonomic, subjective).

questions.question: Order the typical developmental milestones for theory of mind:
items: Metarepresentation ('I know my friend thinks the food is at A when it's really at B')
Pass false-belief tasks (~age 4–5)
Newborn shows basic emotion expression
Fail explicit false-belief tasks (younger children)
correct_order: Newborn shows basic emotion expression
Fail explicit false-belief tasks (younger children)
Pass false-belief tasks (~age 4–5)
Metarepresentation ('I know my friend thinks the food is at A when it's really at B')
questions.explanation: ToM development: babies express basic emotions innately → younger children fail explicit false-belief tasks → around age 4-5 they pass them → metarepresentation (representing another's representations) is a higher-level skill that requires further cognitive maturation.

questions.question: Order the cutting-edge retroactive emotional memory paper paradigm:
items: Surprise associative memory test for original face-object pairs
Initial learning: neutral face-object pair associations
Emotional learning: faces paired with screaming or neutral voice (object no longer shown)
Memory enhanced for faces paired with screaming voice; reactivation in hippocampus + neocortex
correct_order: Initial learning: neutral face-object pair associations
Emotional learning: faces paired with screaming or neutral voice (object no longer shown)
Surprise associative memory test for original face-object pairs
Memory enhanced for faces paired with screaming voice; reactivation in hippocampus + neocortex
questions.explanation: Paradigm: initial neutral face-object learning → emotional learning pairing each face with screaming or neutral voice (no objects) → surprise memory test → memory is retroactively enhanced for screaming-paired faces via amygdala-modulated hippocampal-neocortical reactivation.

questions.question: Order the chimpanzee theory-of-mind paradigm steps:
items: If subordinate takes dominant's perspective: preferentially go for the cache the dominant cannot see
Subordinate observes which cache is occluded from the dominant
Two food caches placed; dominant can see one but not the other
Result: chimps reliably prefer the occluded cache → evidence for rudimentary mentalizing
correct_order: Two food caches placed; dominant can see one but not the other
Subordinate observes which cache is occluded from the dominant
If subordinate takes dominant's perspective: preferentially go for the cache the dominant cannot see
Result: chimps reliably prefer the occluded cache → evidence for rudimentary mentalizing
questions.explanation: Chimp ToM paradigm: two caches with one occluded from the dominant → subordinate observes the occlusion → if it can take the dominant's perspective, it goes for the hidden cache (exploiting dominant's ignorance) → chimps reliably do this, evidence for at least implicit mentalizing.
